title: An Ominous Beginning
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-09-25 -AM- An Ominous Beginning PART 1 - Hosea 1_2-9 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
02-09-25 -PM- An Ominous Beginning PART 2 - Hosea 1_2-9 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction and Historical Context (AM)
subpoints: Hosea's prophecy spans approximately 50 years, from 760-710 BC, during the northern kingdom of Israel
Historical timeline: Solomon to 931 BC; split kingdom; Jeroboam II (800-750 BC) — a time of great prosperity; Hosea begins prophesying 760 BC
Key dates: 746 — Shallum kills Zechariah (Jehu's line ends); 722 — Assyria takes Northern Kingdom; 701 — miraculous deliverance of Judah; 605-587 — Babylonian captivity of Judah
Hosea was likely a young man (possibly a teenager) when he began prophesying

outline.title: 1. Hosea's Marriage Represents God's Relationship to Israel
subpoints: Hosea and Gomer were real historical people — supported by proper names, genealogical detail, and historical context
Gomer was a woman of harlotry; the marriage was a symbolic (not allegorical) account — something that really happened with the purpose of teaching us about God
Most likely interpretation: God commanded Hosea to marry a woman who was already a prostitute, to redeem her — as God called Israel out of idolatry
Application 1: We are always communicating something about Christ with our lives — truth or a lie about Him (Phil. 2:14-16)
Application 2: Sometimes obedience to God brings humiliation in the eyes of the world — better to be humiliated by the world than disobedient to God (Mark 8:38)
Application 3: When God speaks in His Word, we do it right away — divine strength comes in the doing, not before (Phil. 2:12-13)
Application 4: Parents must teach children first-time obedience, so they understand what God expects

outline.title: 2. Israel's Spiritual Prostitution
subpoints: Gomer's prostitution represents Israel's spiritual prostitution — consulting wooden sticks as idols, offering sacrifices on high places
Application 5: We too are prone to wander — we may not bow to wooden idols but may trust in wealth, appearance, or political power rather than God
The call: keep on coming to God — Isaiah 55, Hosea 6:1, Matthew 11:28-30

outline.title: 3. God Judges Disobedience: The Children and Their Names (AM/PM)
subpoints: Three children of Hosea and Gomer are given symbolic names; the other two may have been children of Gomer's prostitution that Hosea adopted
Jezreel (son): named after the valley and city where Jehu committed his bloody atrocities; God promised to judge Jehu's dynasty there — fulfilled in 746 when Shallum killed Zechariah
Lo-Ruhamah (daughter): 'no mercy' — God will no longer have mercy on the house of Israel
Lo-Ammi (son): 'not my people' — God effectively divorces the Northern Kingdom, fulfilled at the Assyrian conquest of 722
Israel's disobedience brought political instability: after Jehu's dynasty ended, the Northern Kingdom had kings for months at a time — unable to resist Assyria

outline.title: 4. A Ray of Hope: Judah Preserved (PM)
subpoints: Verse 7: God will have mercy on the house of Judah — not through military might but by the Lord their God directly
Fulfilled in 701 BC: one angel destroys the entire Assyrian army encircling Jerusalem under Hezekiah, without Israel lifting a finger

outline.title: Application 6: Our Hope Is in the Lord, Not in Political Power (PM)
subpoints: Israel's trust in its army and economy, apart from God, brought divine judgment
In 2025, great political shift rightward across 49 US states — reason for gratitude but not ultimate hope
The church must not make any political figure a savior-like figure — our only Savior is Jesus Christ
Psalm 20:7: 'Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we remember the name of the Lord our God'
The power to change the nation is in the gospel proclaimed from pulpits, not in Washington

summary: In two sermons (AM and PM) covering Hosea 1:2-9, Pastor Tito Lyro establishes the historical context of Hosea's prophecy (eighth-century Northern Kingdom) and expounds the symbolic reality of Hosea's marriage to Gomer: a real event designed to represent God's relationship to spiritually adulterous Israel. He draws six applications from the passage: that we always communicate truth or falsehood about Christ; that obedience sometimes brings humiliation; that God gives grace in the doing; that children need first-time obedience training; that we too are prone to wander and must keep coming to God; and (in the PM) that our hope must be in God, not in political figures or parties. He also expounds the symbolic names of the three children — Jezreel, Lo-Ruhamah, Lo-Ammi — as predictions of God's judgment on Israel's spiritual adultery, while noting the ray of hope in verse 7 (Judah's miraculous deliverance from Assyria in 701 BC).
transcript:
# Part 1 — AM Service

## Introduction and Historical Context

Often books start with an introduction that eases the reader into the story — something like 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.' Not Hosea. He goes directly to the point, providing an ominous beginning to his prophecy. There is no dramatic call to the prophetic office like Isaiah's vision of the throne room, or Jeremiah's conversation with the Lord, or Ezekiel's vision by the river Chebar. When God started speaking to Hosea, it was to tell him to marry a prostitute. That is how it begins.

Hosea was a real man who lived in the eighth century BC. His prophecies addressed the northern kingdom — the ten tribes that rebelled against Rehoboam and established their own state. Because of their size they kept the name Israel; the southern kingdom became known as Judah. The northern kingdom is sometimes called Ephraim (for its largest tribe) or Samaria (for its capital).

Hosea delivered his prophecy over approximately fifty years — likely from 760 BC to beyond 710 BC. He may have been a young man, perhaps even a teenager, when his prophecy begins.

**Key dates:**
- 931 BC — Solomon's death; kingdom splits under Rehoboam and Jeroboam
- 800-750 BC — Jeroboam II reigns in Israel; the longest-reigning northern king; a period of great prosperity
- 760 BC — Hosea begins prophesying
- 746 BC — Shallum kills Zechariah (Jehu's grandson), fulfilling the prophecy in this passage
- 722 BC — Assyria conquers the Northern Kingdom
- 701 BC — God miraculously destroys Sennacherib's army outside Jerusalem
- 605-587 BC — Babylonian captivity of Judah

This is a period of great judgment — most of the writing prophets address it — and yet through every judgment there runs a ray of hope. That is how God works through the prophets.

## 1. Hosea's Marriage Represents God's Relationship to Israel

> "The Lord said to Hosea: 'Go, take yourself a wife of harlotry and children of harlotry, for the land has committed great harlotry by departing from the Lord.'" (Hosea 1:2)

The idea that God commanded a prophet to marry a prostitute has troubled Bible scholars since the earliest centuries. Several alternative interpretations have been proposed. Some say this is an allegorical account — no real Hosea, no real Gomer. But there is no textual indication of allegory. Both Hosea and Gomer were common names for that period. And there is genealogical information: Hosea son of Beeri, Gomer daughter of Diblaim — indicators that the Holy Spirit intends these as real historical persons.

So Hosea's marriage is not allegorical but *symbolic* — something that really happened, carrying the purpose of teaching us about God's relationship to Israel.

Two interpretations remain: either God commanded Hosea to marry a woman who *would* become a prostitute (though not yet one at the wedding), or He commanded Hosea to marry a woman *already* in prostitution in order to redeem her. The text's parallel to God's dealings with Israel favors the second: God called Israel *out of* idolatry to redeem them — just as Hosea redeemed Gomer from her life of prostitution, though she returned to it.

The word translated 'harlotry' is not the term for a professional temple prostitute (typically a slave). Gomer was an Israelite, a member of the covenant community, who of her own will committed adultery with multiple partners. This is exactly what Israel was doing to God.

### Application 1: We Are Always Communicating Something About Christ

Our lives are not divinely inspired Scripture, but we bear the name of Christ. Every second we are communicating either truth or falsehood about Him. Philippians 2:14-16: *Do all things without complaining... that you may shine as lights in the world.* We are all shining — the question is whether we are shining brightly or dimly, accurately or falsely.

### Application 2: Obedience May Bring Humiliation

Consider the humiliation of Hosea's marriage. All the hushed comments: *there is the prophet of God, look at his wife*. And yet Hosea did exactly what God commanded. Mark 8:38: *Whoever is ashamed of Me and My words... of him the Son of Man also will be ashamed when He comes.* It is better to be humiliated by the world than to be disobedient to God. The fear of man is a snare (Proverbs 29:25). Trust in the Lord — that is where safety is.

### Application 3: Obey Right Away

Notice the text: God commands in verse 2; Hosea obeys in verse 3 — immediately. God gives grace in the *doing* of what He calls us to do, not before. Philippians 2:12-13: *Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you.* As you are doing what God calls you to, He is working in you to do it. You do not have to wait for a special feeling or circumstance.

### Application 4: Teach Children First-Time Obedience

Hosea's immediate obedience illustrates what God expects — and what parents must train their children to do. Your children have one job given to them by God: to honor and obey you. When you do not require that, you are opposing God's grace in your child's life. What your child understands about God's expectations of them will come largely through their relationship with you.

## 2. Israel's Spiritual Prostitution

Gomer's prostitution represents Israel's spiritual prostitution. Hosea 4:11-15 describes it: consulting wooden walking sticks as oracles, burning incense on the hills, offering sacrifices on the high places — 'The spirit of harlotry has caused them to stray, and they have played the harlot against their God.'

We look at Israel and wonder *how could they?* — and forget that we too are prone to wander. We may not bow to wooden idols. We may be more sophisticated. But we may look for satisfaction in the things of this world — in wealth, physical appearance, health, or political power — just as Israel did.

### Application 5: Keep Coming to God

The prophets repeatedly call God's people back: 'Come, and let us return to the Lord' (Hosea 6:1). Isaiah 55:1-3: *Everyone who thirsts, come to the waters.* Matthew 11:28-30: *Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.* This is not only for unbelievers. You who are believers and are prone to wander — Christ says, keep coming. He will never turn away anyone who comes to Him.

---

# Part 2 — PM Service

## Review

This morning we saw that Hosea's marriage to Gomer was a real, historical, symbolic account representing God's relationship to Israel — and that Gomer's prostitution represented Israel's spiritual adultery. We drew five applications: we always communicate truth or falsehood about Christ; obedience may bring humiliation; we obey immediately; we teach our children first-time obedience; and we too are prone to wander and must keep coming to God.

This afternoon we continue with verses 3-5 and then look ahead to verses 6-9.

## 3. God Judges Disobedience: The Children and Their Names

Each of the three children of Hosea and Gomer is given a symbolic name. Some scholars note that only the first child — Jezreel — is explicitly said to be *borne to him* (Hosea 1:3). The other two are simply born; the text does not use that clause. This has led scholars to suggest that Lo-Ruhamah and Lo-Ammi were children of Gomer's prostitution whom Hosea adopted and raised — just as God continued to care for Israel even when she was spiritually unfaithful.

### Jezreel

Naming your son Jezreel would be like naming your son Auschwitz or Gettysburg — it pointed to a place of great bloodshed and judgment. Jezreel was the valley where King Jehu carried out his commission against the house of Ahab — and went far beyond it. God told Jehu to eliminate Ahab's lineage. He did — and then continued killing far beyond what God had authorized, and he continued to worship false gods (2 Kings 9-10). God's response: 'Because you did what I commanded regarding Ahab, your dynasty will last four generations. But because you exceeded what I commanded and continued in idolatry, your line will be cut off.'

Fulfilled in 746 BC — Shallum killed Zechariah, Jehu's grandson, at Jezreel, ending the dynasty. The end of Jehu's house brought thirty years of political instability: king after king, assassinated after months in power, until the Assyrians arrived and took the weakened nation captive in 722 BC.

Jezreel's name told Israel during a time of great prosperity — under Jeroboam II, with a strong economy, a powerful army, secure borders — that their trust in their king, their army, and their national wealth was misplaced. Judgment was coming.

### Lo-Ruhamah

'No mercy' — God will no longer have mercy on the house of Israel.

### Lo-Ammi

'Not my people' — 'For you are not My people, and I will not be your God.' This is the language of divorce. God effectively ends the covenant relationship with the northern kingdom. Fulfilled in 722 BC when the Assyrians took them captive.

## 4. A Ray of Hope

> "Yet I will have mercy on the house of Judah, and will save them by the LORD their God, and will not save them by bow, sword or battle, by horses or horsemen." (Hosea 1:7)

Even in the darkness of judgment, there is a ray of hope. God will remain faithful to the remnant in Judah — and He will save them not by military power but by His direct intervention. Fulfilled in 701 BC: one angel of the Lord destroys Sennacherib's entire army encircling Jerusalem, without Judah lifting a finger.

## Application 6: Our Hope Is in the Lord, Not in Political Power

Israel's trust in its army, its economy, and its government — apart from God — led to divine judgment. This is a warning for us.

In the 2025 election, forty-nine states moved rightward politically. Washington State was the only exception. This is reason to be grateful — it is not a bad thing when the political climate becomes more favorable to Christian ethics. But we must be very careful that our hope is not in a political party or in a president.

In the church of Jesus Christ, there has been a troubling tendency to portray political figures in almost messianic terms — on the right as much as on the left. The problem is that no human figure can do the job that only the God-man can do. Our trust must not be in the president, nor in the system of government, nor in the political party. Psalm 20:7: *Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we will remember the name of the Lord our God.* Psalm 124:8: *Our help is in the name of the Lord, who made heaven and earth.*

We may be more politically conservative than before the election. We are still a nation that needs salvation, a nation that has driven the presence of God from the public square, a nation under the providential judgment of God. No president can reverse that. Only the gospel of Jesus Christ can. The power is not in Washington. It is in the pulpits of the land, where Christ must be proclaimed.

So what do we do? We rejoice where things move in a more favorable direction. We vote. We advocate. And above all, we remember: our hope is in the Lord who made heaven and earth, not in the elections of men.
